title: lithospermum fruticosum var. prostratum
intro: Lithospermum fruticosum var. prostratum, commonly known as creeping germander, is a perennial plant in the borage family (Boraginaceae). This plant has been traditionally used for its health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it useful for treating various health conditions such as wounds, skin issues, and respiratory problems. The therapeutic actions of creeping germander include its ability to soothe and calm the skin, reduce inflammation, and exhibit antimicrobial activity, making it an effective remedy for various skin and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of creeping germander include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties and health benefits.

parts: Lithospermum fruticosum var. prostratum has many medicinal parts, such as the roots, leaves, flowers, and whole plant. The roots of Lithospermum fruticosum var. prostratum are known to contain alkaloids, including lithospermic acid and caffeic acid, which have been traditionally used to treat various health conditions. The leaves and flowers of the plant are rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, including kaempferol and quercetin, which possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The whole plant is also a source of iridoid glycosides, including lithospermic acid, which has been used to treat fever, sore throat, and other infections.

side_effects: Lithospermum fruticosum var. prostratum can have side effects if used improperly, such as gastrointestinal upset, nausea, and diarrhea. Prolonged or excessive use of the plant can also lead to kidney damage and increased risk of bleeding. Additionally, it may interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, and decrease their effectiveness.
